name: Residential Environment Design
consequences: Immediate: Adjusting padding and window restrictions reduces injury risk → Systemic: 15% decrease in false alarm rates due to improved participant compliance → Strategic: Enhances long-term facility adoption but may compromise domestic comfort, risking underrepresentation of natural behavior patterns.
options: Implement standardized safety protocols with periodic third-party audits
Integrate eco-friendly materials and biophilic design to improve participant retention
Deploy modular, IoT-enabled safety systems with real-time anomaly detection
review: Controls clinical safety vs. behavioral authenticity. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term maintenance costs of adaptive technologies.

name: Staffing and Coverage Model
consequences: Immediate: Rotating night shifts increases technician fatigue → Systemic: 20% higher annotation error rates due to cognitive load → Strategic: Risks data integrity but maintains 24/7 operational continuity under current staffing ratios.
options: Adopt fixed 12-hour shifts with mandatory rest periods between rotations
Introduce cross-training for technicians to handle multiple suite clusters
Integrate remote monitoring via AI-assisted alert triage systems
review: Controls operational continuity vs. human error. Weakness: The options neglect the psychological impact of prolonged night work on staff retention.

name: Sensor Integration Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Full PSG montages increase participant discomfort → Systemic: 30% reduction in event capture rates due to compliance issues → Strategic: Improves physiological resolution but limits longitudinal data consistency.
options: Prioritize low-burden sensors for baseline monitoring only
Schedule enhanced PSG nights during peak event likelihood windows
Test non-invasive thermal imaging for movement pattern analysis
review: Controls data fidelity vs. participant burden. Weakness: The options overlook the trade-off between sensor complexity and real-time annotation accuracy.

name: Participant Selection and Exclusion Criteria
consequences: Immediate: Strict epilepsy exclusion raises recruitment barriers → Systemic: 25% lower enrollment rates in year one due to specialist referral bottlenecks → Strategic: Ensures clinical specificity but delays phenotyping timelines by 6–12 months.
options: Expand eligibility to include self-reported epilepsy history with neurologist review
Implement community-based screening via local sleep clinics and GPs
Use machine learning-driven risk stratification for preliminary eligibility
review: Controls clinical precision vs. recruitment speed. Weakness: The options fail to account for regional healthcare access disparities affecting referral rates.

consequences: Immediate: Restricted video deposition limits algorithm training data → Systemic: 40% slower tool benchmarking due to manual annotation dependencies → Strategic: Protects participant rights but hinders collaboration with external researchers.

review: Controls ethical compliance vs. analytical scalability. Weakness: The options neglect the potential for synthetic data generation to mitigate privacy constraints.
